narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 37, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and calculated a 91.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this to prioritize candidates likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile of 1, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it signals consistently lower performance relative to peers in later undergraduate years. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, compared to an average of about 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early academic struggles that often correlate with bar exam challenges. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 37—slightly above the average of 36.7—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction somewhat toward passing and showing your potential in standardized testing. While your full-time status aligns with most applicants (over 90%) and your GPA of 3.10 is close to the average of 3.21, those lower deciles stood out as red flags compared to typical successful students who rank higher in their school cohorts. This isn't a judgment on your character or background, but a data-driven assessment focused on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your academic deciles, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA improvement initiative, and reapply after demonstrating stronger performance. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your LSAT strength gives you a solid foundation to build on. With focused effort, you can shift the odds in your favor for future applications.
